text:
The recovery of uranium from wet phosphoric acid (WPA) serves as a vital strategy for diversifying the global uranium supply, particularly as demand for nuclear energy grows in developing economies. While industrial-scale production of uranium from phosphate rocks was successfully implemented between 1951 and 1999, these operations eventually ceased due to unfavorable market fluctuations. This review provides a comprehensive analysis of the historical solvent extraction processes—specifically the Oak Ridge (D2EHPA/TOPO), OPAP, and OPPA methods—detailing their technical frameworks, industrial implementation, and the fundamental physicochemistry that governed their performance. 

Beyond historical context, the paper evaluates recent advancements in extractant chemistry aimed at overcoming perennial challenges such as insufficient selectivity against iron(III) and reduced efficiency in concentrated phosphoric acid. Emerging extraction systems and the development of bifunctional ligands, such as alkylcarbamoylnonyl phosphonates, are highlighted for their potential to significantly enhance distribution ratios and purity levels. The review also examines alternative technologies, including ion exchange resins and the co-recovery of rare earth elements, to improve the overall economic viability of WPA processing. By synthesizing past lessons with modern chemical innovations, this work outlines a roadmap for more cost-effective and environmentally responsible uranium recovery in a shifting energy landscape.
